title: Lines & Planes in Space
blurb: The rules of 3D: how planes are determined, how lines can miss each other without being parallel, and how to measure distances and angles when things leave the flat page.
buildsOn: Plane geometry — angles, parallel lines, the Pythagorean theorem, right-triangle trigonometry.

lessons.title: Planes & How They Meet
lessons.concreteComparison: A camera tripod never wobbles, but a four-legged chair often does. Three feet always land on ONE flat plane — that's the first axiom of space working for you. The chair's fourth leg has no such guarantee, so it hovers.
lessons.objective: Use the axioms of space: what determines a plane, and the three ways a line or another plane can meet it.
concept: In space, the new object is the **plane** — an infinite flat sheet. The first rule: **three points not on one line determine exactly one plane.** That's why a tripod (3 feet) is always steady and a 4-legged chair can wobble: the 4th foot may miss the plane of the other three.

A **line and a plane** can meet in exactly three ways: the line lies IN the plane (every point shared), the line **pierces** it at exactly one point, or the line is **parallel** to it (no shared point). There is no fourth option — two shared points already drag the whole line into the plane.

**Two planes** either are parallel (never meet) or intersect — and when they intersect, the intersection is always a **straight line**, never just a point. Look at any wall meeting the floor: the crease is a line. Every 3D figure we build is stitched together along such lines.
lessons.keyIdea: Three non-collinear points fix a plane; a line meets a plane in 0, 1, or infinitely many points; two non-parallel planes always meet in a line.

facts.title: Determining a plane
facts.explanation: Also: a line + a point off it, or two intersecting lines.

facts.title: Line meets plane
facts.explanation: Parallel, piercing, or lying in the plane — no other case.

facts.title: Plane meets plane
facts.explanation: Intersecting planes always share a whole LINE.

workedExamples.statement: How many distinct planes are determined by $4$ points in space, no $3$ of them collinear and not all $4$ in one plane?
workedExamples.solution: Every choice of 3 points gives one plane, and the conditions make all these planes distinct: $\binom{4}{3} = 4$ planes. (That's exactly a triangular pyramid: 4 vertices, 4 triangular faces.)

workedExamples.statement: A cube has $6$ faces. Each pair of ADJACENT faces meets in an edge (a line segment). Count the edges by counting adjacent face-pairs.
workedExamples.solution: Each of the 6 faces touches 4 neighbors, giving $6 \cdot 4 = 24$ face-pair contacts — but each edge got counted from both sides, so there are $\frac{24}{2} = 12$ edges. This matches the cube's known 12 edges: intersecting planes meet in lines, and the cube's edges are exactly those intersection lines cut to size.

commonMistakes.text: Thinking two planes can intersect in a single point, the way two lines do.
commonMistakes.correction: Planes are too big for that. If planes share one point, they must share a whole line through it. Only a LINE and a plane can meet in a single point.

commonMistakes.text: Assuming any 3 points determine a plane — forgetting the collinear exception.
commonMistakes.correction: Three points ON one line lie in infinitely many planes (the pages of an open book all contain its spine). The determination rule needs three points NOT on one line.

tryIt.statement: Points $A, B, C, D, E$ in space, no 3 collinear, no 4 coplanar. How many planes do they determine?
tryIt.solution: Each 3-point choice gives a distinct plane: $\binom{5}{3} = 10$ planes.

tryIt.statement: A line pierces a plane. In how many points do they meet? What if the line lay in the plane?
tryIt.solution: Piercing: exactly $1$ point. Lying in the plane: infinitely many (every point of the line). The impossible count is exactly $2$: two shared points force the whole line into the plane.

steps.title: A plane is an infinite flat sheet
steps.body: Plane geometry lived inside ONE flat sheet. Solid geometry stacks many such sheets in space. We draw a plane as a parallelogram (you're seeing a rectangle tilted away from you) and name it with a Greek letter like $\alpha$. In the figure, points $A$ and $B$ lie in the plane; $P$ floats above it.

steps.title: Three points make a plane — the tripod rule
steps.body: Through any **three points not on one line** passes exactly ONE plane. Fewer points aren't enough: through one point pass infinitely many planes; through two points (a line) still infinitely many — think of the pages of a book turning around its spine. Three non-collinear points $A$, $B$, $C$ nail the sheet down: that's why tripods never wobble.

steps.title: Why tripods and not chairs?
steps.prompt: A table with $3$ legs stands firm on any floor; one with $4$ legs can wobble. Which fact explains this?
options: Three non-collinear points determine exactly one plane
Two planes intersect in a line
A line and a plane meet in at most one point unless the line lies in the plane
Four points always lie in one plane
steps.explanation: Any 3 leg-tips land in SOME plane — the tripod settles into it. A 4th tip has no guarantee of lying in the plane of the first three (option D is false!), so one leg can hover.

steps.title: Three ways a line can meet a plane
steps.body: Count shared points. **In the plane**: infinitely many. **Piercing**: exactly one — the line stabs through at point $M$ like a pencil through paper. **Parallel**: zero. The figure shows a piercing line: the part below the plane is dashed, because the sheet hides it from view. There's no 'exactly two' case: two shared points pull the entire line into the plane.

steps.title: Two planes meet in a LINE
steps.body: If two planes share even one point, they share a whole straight line — the crease $\ell$ where wall meets floor, cover meets page. (They can't share just a point: planes are infinite in every direction, so an overlap that small is impossible.) The only alternative is that they never meet at all: **parallel planes**, like ceiling and floor.

steps.title: Counting planes
steps.intro: The determination rule turns into pure counting.

examples.prompt: 4 points, no 3 collinear, not all coplanar. How many planes?
steps: $\binom{4}{3} = 4$ — and the conditions keep them distinct.
examples.answer: $4$ planes (the faces of a triangular pyramid)

examples.prompt: How many planes pass through one given line?
steps: Rotate a sheet around the line like pages around a spine.
Every rotation position is a different plane.

steps.title: Axioms in action
steps.intro: Think in shared points: 0, 1, or infinitely many.

problems.prompt: 5 points, no 3 collinear, no 4 coplanar. How many planes do they determine?
A line shares exactly $2$ points with a plane. What follows?
options: The line lies entirely in the plane
The line pierces the plane twice
The line is parallel to the plane
problems.explanation: Two shared points force EVERY point of the line into the plane — so really it shares infinitely many, not just 2. A line can't pierce a plane twice (planes are flat, lines are straight).

problems.prompt: Can two planes intersect in exactly one point?
options: No — if they share a point, they share a whole line
Yes, if they touch at a corner
problems.explanation: Plane∩plane is all-or-line: either no common points (parallel) or a full straight line of them. 'Touching at a corner' is a polygon phenomenon, not a plane one.

steps.title: The ground rules of space
bullets: 3 non-collinear points ⟹ exactly 1 plane (tripod rule); also line + outside point, or two intersecting lines.
Line vs plane: 0 points (parallel), 1 point (pierces), or ∞ (lies inside) — never exactly 2.
Plane vs plane: parallel, or they meet in a straight LINE (the crease).
Dashed lines in figures = hidden behind a plane; solid = visible.

lessons.title: Parallel & Skew Lines
lessons.concreteComparison: A highway overpass: the road below and the road above never meet — but they're NOT parallel, they point in completely different directions. Lines that miss each other without being parallel exist only in 3D. That's what makes space genuinely different from a flat map.
lessons.objective: Classify pairs of lines in space (intersecting, parallel, skew) and recognize when a line is parallel to a plane.
concept: On a flat page, two lines either cross or are parallel. Space adds a third option: **skew lines** — lines that never meet AND aren't parallel, because they live in different planes. The overpass and the road beneath it: no intersection, different directions. Skew lines are the signature of 3D.

The test: parallel lines must lie **in one plane** and never meet. Skew lines lie in NO common plane. On a cube, edge $AB$ (bottom front) and edge $B'C'$ (top right, running away from you) are skew: extend them forever, they neither touch nor point the same way.

**Line parallel to a plane**: a line that never meets the plane. The working criterion — if line $\ell$ (outside plane $\alpha$) is parallel to SOME line inside $\alpha$, then $\ell \parallel \alpha$. A curtain rod is parallel to the floor because it's parallel to the floor's baseboard line.
lessons.keyIdea: In space, non-meeting lines split into parallel (same plane, same direction) and skew (no common plane). A line is parallel to a plane iff it's parallel to a line inside it.

facts.title: Skew lines
facts.explanation: No meeting, no common plane — only possible in 3D.

facts.title: Parallel transitivity
facts.latex: a \parallel b, \; b \parallel c \Rightarrow a \parallel c
facts.explanation: Holds in space just as on the page.

facts.title: Line ∥ plane
facts.explanation: Parallel to one line of the plane ⟹ parallel to the plane.

workedExamples.statement: In cube $ABCDA'B'C'D'$, classify each pair: (a) $AB$ and $A'B'$; (b) $AB$ and $CC'$; (c) $AB$ and $BC$.
workedExamples.solution: (a) $A'B'$ is $AB$ shifted straight up — **parallel**. (b) $AB$ runs along the bottom front; $CC'$ is the vertical edge at the back-right. They never meet and point in different directions — **skew**. (c) $AB$ and $BC$ share corner $B$ — **intersecting** (at a right angle, since a cube's face is a square: $90°$).

workedExamples.statement: A cube has $12$ edges. Pick edge $AB$. Of the other $11$ edges, how many are parallel to $AB$, how many intersect it, and how many are skew to it?
workedExamples.solution: Parallel: the 3 other edges running the same direction — $DC$, $A'B'$, $D'C'$. Intersecting: the 4 edges through $A$ or through $B$ — $AD$, $AA'$, $BC$, $BB'$. Skew: the remaining $11 - 3 - 4 = 4$ — the verticals $CC'$, $DD'$ and the depth edges $A'D'$, $B'C'$. Count check: $3 + 4 + 4 = 11$ ✓.

commonMistakes.correction: Parallel needs BOTH: never meeting AND lying in one common plane (same direction). Non-meeting lines with different directions are skew. In space, 'they don't touch' is not enough evidence for parallel.

commonMistakes.text: Trusting the drawing: two segments that cross on PAPER must intersect in space.
commonMistakes.correction: An oblique drawing flattens depth — skew lines often LOOK like they cross. Verify with structure (do they lie in a common face?), not with the picture. In a cube drawing, $AB'$ and $A'B$ genuinely cross (both lie in face $ABB'A'$); $AB'$ and $DC'$ only appear to.

tryIt.statement: In the cube, edge $AA'$ is vertical. How many of the cube's $12$ edges are parallel to $AA'$?
tryIt.solution: All four vertical edges are parallel; besides $AA'$ itself that's $BB'$, $CC'$, $DD'$ — so $3$ others. The cube's 12 edges split into 3 directions × 4 parallel edges.

tryIt.statement: True or false: if line $\ell$ is parallel to plane $\alpha$, then $\ell$ is parallel to EVERY line in $\alpha$.
tryIt.solution: False. $\ell$ is parallel to some lines of $\alpha$ (the ones with its direction) but SKEW to all the others. A curtain rod is parallel to the floor, yet skew to most floor lines — e.g. any floor line running front-to-back. Being ∥ to the plane only promises no meeting, not shared direction with everything.

steps.title: Meet, run parallel — or miss sideways
steps.body: Flat geometry gives two lines only two choices: cross or be parallel. In space there's a third: **skew** — no meeting point AND no common plane. The cube shows all three at once: $AB$ and $AD$ intersect at $A$; $AB \parallel A'B'$ (front face's top and bottom); $AB$ and $CC'$ are skew — trace them and feel how they slide past each other at different heights and directions. Hidden edges are dashed.

steps.title: Skew = no meeting, no common plane
steps.body: Two lines are **skew** when no single flat sheet contains both. Test with the cube's $AB$ (bottom front, left-right) and $CC'$ (back-right, vertical): any plane containing $AB$ would have to tilt to also swallow the vertical line at the back corner — impossible. Compare: $AB$ and $A'B'$ DO share a plane (the front face), so they're parallel, not skew. **Skew lines are why the overpass never needs a traffic light.**

steps.title: Spot the skew pair
steps.prompt: In the cube, which pair of edges is SKEW?
steps.explanation: $AA'$ is vertical at the front-left; $BC$ runs into the depth at the bottom-right. No common plane, no intersection: skew. ($AB \parallel DC$ — both left-right; $AA' \parallel BB'$ — both vertical; $AB$ and $BC$ meet at $B$.)

steps.title: Parallel to one line inside ⟹ parallel to the plane
steps.body: How do you certify a line never meets a whole infinite plane? Find ONE line inside the plane parallel to yours. In the figure, $\ell$ runs above the plane parallel to $m$ (drawn inside the plane): if $\ell$ ever dropped into the plane it would have to cross $m$'s direction — contradiction, so $\ell \parallel \alpha$. This is the criterion you'll use constantly in prisms: every top edge is parallel to the bottom face.

steps.title: Sorting a cube's edges
steps.intro: Fix one edge; sort the other 11 by relationship.

examples.prompt: Relative to edge $AB$: how many edges are parallel / intersecting / skew?
Is $A'B'$ (top-front edge) parallel to the bottom face $ABCD$?
steps: $A'B' \parallel AB$, and $AB$ lies in the bottom face.
Parallel to a line of the plane ⟹ parallel to the plane.

steps.title: Classify with confidence
steps.intro: Same plane or not — that's always the question.

problems.prompt: Lines $a$ and $b$ never meet. What are the possibilities?
problems.explanation: Never meeting rules out intersecting but leaves both parallel (common plane) and skew (no common plane).

problems.prompt: In the cube, how many edges are SKEW to the vertical edge $AA'$?
problems.explanation: Parallel to $AA'$: 3 (the other verticals). Meeting it: 4 (two at $A$: $AB$, $AD$; two at $A'$: $A'B'$, $A'D'$). Skew: $11 - 3 - 4 = 4$ — the four horizontal edges not touching $A$ or $A'$: $BC$, $DC$, $B'C'$, $D'C'$.

problems.prompt: Line $\ell$ is parallel to plane $\alpha$. Can a line in $\alpha$ be skew to $\ell$?
options: No — all lines of α are parallel to ℓ
problems.explanation: $\ell$ never MEETS any line of $\alpha$ (it never meets $\alpha$ at all), but only the lines sharing its direction are parallel; every other line of $\alpha$ is skew to $\ell$.

steps.title: Skew lines run the city
steps.body: Highway interchanges are skew-line sculptures: each ramp is built so its centerline is skew to the roads it crosses — no intersection, ever, by design. The four-level 'stack' interchanges in big cities hold 4+ mutually skew roadways within a hundred vertical feet. Traffic lights exist only where geometry forces lines to actually intersect.

steps.title: Lines in space
bullets: Three relationships: intersect (1 point), parallel (same plane, no meeting), skew (no plane, no meeting).
Cube edge count from any edge: 3 parallel + 4 intersecting + 4 skew = 11 others.
ℓ ∥ (a line in α) and ℓ ⊄ α ⟹ ℓ ∥ α.
Don't trust apparent crossings in oblique drawings — verify with faces/planes.

lessons.title: Perpendicular to a Plane
lessons.concreteComparison: A flagpole isn't 'mostly straight' — it's checked from TWO sides. Builders sight it from the north and from the east; if it's plumb in both views, it's plumb, period. That's the whole theorem: perpendicular to two directions of the ground means perpendicular to every direction of the ground.
lessons.objective: Use the definition and criterion of line ⊥ plane, and compute distances via the perpendicular-and-oblique right triangle.
concept: A line is **perpendicular to a plane** when it's perpendicular to EVERY line of the plane through its foot. Sounds like infinitely many checks — but the criterion cuts it to two: **perpendicular to two intersecting lines of the plane ⟹ perpendicular to the plane.** (The flagpole checked from two directions.)

From point $P$ above a plane, the **perpendicular** $PF$ is the shortest path down; its length is THE **distance from $P$ to the plane**. Any other segment $PA$ from $P$ to the plane is called an **oblique** — and $\triangle PFA$ is a right triangle with the right angle at $F$: $PA^2 = PF^2 + FA^2$. Every distance problem in this course is this triangle, found and solved.
Longer reach along the ground means longer oblique: obliques with equal feet-distances are equal, and $PA > PF$ always — the perpendicular is unbeatable. This is Pythagoras wearing 3D clothes.
lessons.keyIdea: ⊥ to two intersecting lines of a plane ⟹ ⊥ to the whole plane. Distance from a point = the perpendicular, and perpendicular + oblique always form a right triangle.

facts.title: The criterion
facts.explanation: Two intersecting directions suffice — the flagpole test.

facts.title: Distance to a plane
facts.explanation: The perpendicular is the shortest segment from P to the plane.

facts.title: Oblique triangle
facts.explanation: Perpendicular PF, foot-run FA, oblique PA: right angle at F.

workedExamples.statement: $PF \perp \alpha$ with $PF = 4$. Point $A$ lies in $\alpha$ with $FA = 3$. Find the oblique $PA$.
workedExamples.solution: Right triangle at $F$: $PA = \sqrt{PF^2 + FA^2} = \sqrt{16 + 9} = \sqrt{25} = 5$. The 3-4-5 triangle, standing upright in space.

workedExamples.statement: From point $P$, two obliques of length $10$ and an unknown one reach plane $\alpha$. The perpendicular is $PF = 6$. The feet of the length-10 obliques are how far from $F$? And how long is an oblique whose foot is $2\sqrt{7}$ from $F$?
workedExamples.solution: Foot distance for length 10: $FA = \sqrt{10^2 - 6^2} = \sqrt{64} = 8$. (Both length-10 obliques land on the circle of radius 8 around $F$.) For $FB = 2\sqrt7$: $PB = \sqrt{36 + 28} = \sqrt{64} = 8$. Notice $8 < 10$: shorter run along the ground, shorter oblique.

commonMistakes.text: Checking perpendicularity against just ONE line of the plane and concluding ⊥ to the plane.
commonMistakes.correction: One check isn't plumb: a leaning flagpole can still look vertical from one side. You need two INTERSECTING lines of the plane. (Two parallel lines don't work either — they're the same direction twice.)

commonMistakes.text: Taking an oblique segment as 'the distance' to the plane.
commonMistakes.correction: Distance means the perpendicular — the shortest. If a problem says 'distance from P to α is 6', that 6 is $PF$ with $PF \perp \alpha$, and every other reach $PA$ is strictly longer.

tryIt.statement: $PF \perp \alpha$, $PF = 12$, oblique $PA = 13$. How far is $A$ from the foot $F$?
From $P$, the perpendicular to $\alpha$ is $8$ and an oblique is $17$. Find the oblique's foot-distance, and decide: can another oblique from $P$ have length $7$?
tryIt.solution: $FA = \sqrt{17^2 - 8^2} = \sqrt{289 - 64} = \sqrt{225} = 15$ (8-15-17 triple). No oblique can be $7$: every oblique is LONGER than the perpendicular $8$.

steps.title: Perpendicular to a plane = perpendicular to everything in it
steps.body: $PF \perp \alpha$ means: at the foot $F$, the line $PF$ makes a right angle with EVERY line of the plane through $F$. In the picture, $PF$ is vertical and two plane lines run through $F$; both meet $PF$ at $90°$. The mast-and-deck picture is the one to keep: a mast isn't perpendicular to one plank, it's perpendicular to the deck.

steps.title: Two checks are enough
steps.body: Infinitely many lines pass through $F$ — but you only test two. **If $\ell \perp a$ and $\ell \perp b$ where $a$ and $b$ are two INTERSECTING lines of the plane, then $\ell \perp \alpha$.** Why two? Two intersecting directions span the whole plane — every other direction is a mix of those two, and perpendicularity survives the mixing. One direction is never enough: a tilted pole can hide its lean from a single viewpoint.

steps.title: The flagpole inspector
steps.prompt: A pole meets the ground at $90°$ measured against the east–west chalk line. What ELSE must you check to certify the pole ⊥ ground?
options: A second, intersecting ground line — e.g. the north–south line
Nothing — one right angle is enough
Every ground line through the foot, one by one
A line parallel to the east–west line
steps.explanation: Two intersecting directions span the ground plane, so two checks certify all of them. One check can be fooled by a lean in the unchecked direction, and a PARALLEL second line adds no new direction at all.

steps.title: Perpendicular & oblique — the master right triangle
steps.body: Drop the perpendicular $PF$ (the **distance**), then reach out to any point $A$ of the plane with the **oblique** $PA$. Since $PF \perp \alpha$, the angle at $F$ is right, and $\triangle PFA$ is drawn here in TRUE shape: $PF = 4$, $FA = 3$, so $PA = \sqrt{4^2 + 3^2} = 5$. Height, run, slant — every 3D distance problem reduces to finding this triangle.

steps.title: Pythagoras, standing up
steps.intro: Perpendicular = one leg, ground-run = other leg, oblique = hypotenuse.

examples.prompt: Distance $PF = 6$; an oblique has length $10$. How far along the plane is its foot from $F$?
steps: Now the oblique is the hypotenuse: $FA = \sqrt{10^2 - 6^2}$.

steps.title: The perpendicular always wins
steps.body: Among ALL segments from $P$ to the plane, the perpendicular is strictly shortest — it's a leg while every oblique is a hypotenuse. And obliques sort by their ground-runs: farther foot, longer oblique; equal runs, equal obliques (their feet trace a circle around $F$). So 'distance to the plane' is well-defined: there's exactly one minimal reach, and it's the plumb drop.

steps.title: Drop, run, reach
steps.intro: Identify which of the three lengths you have; Pythagoras does the rest.

problems.prompt: Perpendicular $8$; two obliques run to feet $6$ and $15$ from $F$. Their lengths?
problems.explanation: $\sqrt{64+36} = 10$ and $\sqrt{64+225} = 17$ — two Pythagorean triples sharing the leg 8. Farther foot ⟹ longer oblique ✓.

problems.prompt: Can an oblique from $P$ be SHORTER than the perpendicular from $P$?
options: Never — the perpendicular is the minimum
Yes, if the foot is very close to F
problems.explanation: The oblique is the hypotenuse of a right triangle in which the perpendicular is a leg — hypotenuse > leg, always. As the foot approaches $F$ the oblique approaches $PF$ but never dips below it.

steps.title: Perpendicularity toolkit
bullets: ℓ ⊥ α means ⊥ to every plane line through the foot; certify it with just TWO intersecting lines.
Distance from P to α = the perpendicular PF — the unique shortest segment.
Perpendicular + oblique ⟹ right triangle at the foot: PA² = PF² + FA².
Memorize the triple family: 3-4-5, 6-8-10, 5-12-13, 8-15-17 — solid geometry runs on them.

lessons.title: Angles in Space
lessons.concreteComparison: A ladder against a wall is safe at about 75° and lethal at 40° — but 75° measured against WHAT? Not the wall, not the rungs: against the ladder's own shadow at noon. The angle between a line and a plane is defined by exactly that shadow, and it's the angle every safety code, ski slope, and flight path is written in.
lessons.objective: Measure the angle between a line and a plane (via the projection) and between two planes (the dihedral angle, via two perpendiculars to the edge).
concept: **Line-plane angle**: drop the perpendicular from a point of the line to get its **projection** (shadow) on the plane; the angle between the line and its own projection is THE angle between line and plane. In the perpendicular-oblique triangle, that's the angle at $A$: $\sin\theta = \frac{PF}{PA} = \frac{\text{height}}{\text{oblique}}$.

**Dihedral angle** (between two planes): stand on the intersection line $\ell$, draw one ray in each plane PERPENDICULAR to $\ell$ at the same point — the angle between those rays is the dihedral angle. An open laptop: the hinge is $\ell$, and the screen-keyboard angle is measured perpendicular to the hinge, not diagonally across.

Both definitions manufacture a flat, true-shape right triangle inside the 3D figure. The whole game of angle problems: FIND that triangle, then it's Unit-1-of-trigonometry — $\sin$, $\cos$, $\tan$ of a right triangle.
lessons.keyIdea: Line-plane angle = angle between the line and its projection (sin θ = height/oblique). Plane-plane angle = angle between two rays ⊥ to the edge, one in each plane.

facts.title: Line ∧ plane
facts.explanation: Height over oblique — the angle sits between oblique and its shadow.

facts.title: Dihedral angle
facts.explanation: Measure square to the hinge, never diagonally.

facts.title: Range
facts.explanation: Line-plane angles never exceed 90° — the shadow definition guarantees it.

workedExamples.statement: An oblique $PA = 6$ meets plane $\alpha$; the perpendicular from $P$ is $PF = 3$. Find the angle between $PA$ and the plane.
workedExamples.solution: $\sin\theta = \frac{PF}{PA} = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}$, so $\theta = 30°$. (The projection has length $FA = \sqrt{36-9} = 3\sqrt3$ — a 30-60-90 triangle.)

workedExamples.statement: At point $M$ on the edge of a dihedral angle, ray $MK \perp$ edge is drawn in plane $\alpha$ and ray $MN \perp$ edge in plane $\beta$, with $MK = MN = 6$ and $KN = 6\sqrt{2}$. Find the dihedral angle.
workedExamples.solution: The angle $\angle KMN$ IS the dihedral angle (both rays ⊥ the edge at $M$). Triangle $KMN$ has sides $6, 6, 6\sqrt2$ — and $6^2 + 6^2 = 72 = (6\sqrt2)^2$, so by the converse of Pythagoras $\angle KMN = 90°$. The planes are perpendicular.

commonMistakes.text: Measuring the line-plane angle between the line and some random line of the plane.
commonMistakes.correction: The definition is specific: line vs its own PROJECTION. Any other plane line gives a bigger angle. Build the projection by dropping the perpendicular first.

commonMistakes.text: Measuring a dihedral angle with rays that aren't perpendicular to the edge.
commonMistakes.correction: Slanted rays exaggerate or shrink the angle — a laptop 'measured' diagonally seems more closed than it is. Both rays must hit the hinge at 90°; only then is the angle between them the dihedral angle.

tryIt.statement: A $10$-unit oblique makes a $45°$ angle with a plane. Find the height (perpendicular) and the projection.
tryIt.solution: Height $= 10\sin 45° = 10 \cdot \frac{\sqrt2}{2} = 5\sqrt{2}$; projection $= 10\cos 45° = 5\sqrt{2}$. At $45°$ they're equal — the perpendicular-oblique triangle is the isosceles half-square.

tryIt.statement: A mast of height $9$ is stabilized by a wire from its top to a ground anchor $9\sqrt{3}$ from its base. What angle does the wire make with the GROUND?

steps.title: A line's angle with a plane is measured against its shadow
steps.body: Sun directly overhead: the oblique $PA$ casts the shadow (projection) $FA$ on the plane. The angle between $PA$ and the plane is $\angle PAF$ — oblique vs shadow. The triangle $PFA$ is drawn in true shape here: height $3$, oblique $6$, so $\sin\theta = \frac{3}{6} = \frac12$ and $\theta = 30°$. Never measure against anything but the projection.

steps.title: sin θ = height / oblique
steps.body: In $\triangle PFA$ the right angle is at the foot $F$. Relative to the angle $\theta$ at $A$: opposite = the height $PF$, hypotenuse = the oblique $PA$. So $\sin\theta = \frac{PF}{PA}$, $\cos\theta = \frac{FA}{PA}$ (shadow over oblique), $\tan\theta = \frac{PF}{FA}$ (height over shadow). Given any two of {height, shadow, oblique, angle}, the other two follow. This one triangle IS the lesson.

steps.title: Read the triangle
steps.prompt: An oblique of length $8$ makes a $60°$ angle with the plane. What's the height $PF$?
steps.explanation: $PF = 8\sin 60° = 8 \cdot \frac{\sqrt3}{2} = 4\sqrt3 \approx 6.9$ — less than the oblique 8 ✓. Option B is $8\cos 60°$: that's the SHADOW, not the height.

steps.title: The dihedral angle: measure square to the hinge
steps.body: Two half-planes meet along edge $\ell$ — a half-open book. To measure how far open: pick a point $M$ on the spine, draw $MK \perp \ell$ on one page and $MN \perp \ell$ on the other. $\angle KMN$ is the **dihedral angle** — and drawn here in true shape, looking straight down the hinge. Rays not perpendicular to the hinge give false readings (that's why the definition insists).

steps.title: Both kinds of angle
steps.intro: Find the true-shape triangle, then it's plain trig.

examples.prompt: Oblique $6$, height $3$. Angle with the plane?
Rays $MK = MN = 6$, both ⊥ the edge at $M$; $KN = 6\sqrt2$. Dihedral angle?
steps: $\angle KMN$ is the dihedral angle (both rays ⊥ edge).

steps.title: Every angle problem is a triangle hunt
steps.body: The 3D drawing is scaffolding; the answer lives in a flat right triangle. Procedure: (1) identify the perpendicular (height) — build it if absent; (2) identify the shadow or the square-to-the-hinge rays; (3) redraw JUST that triangle flat, with its true lengths; (4) apply $\sin/\cos/\tan$. Working flat is not a shortcut — it's the definition doing exactly what it was designed to do.

steps.title: Angle hunting
steps.intro: Height, shadow, oblique — name them, then compute.

problems.prompt: Oblique $10$ at $45°$ to the plane. Height?
Height $9$, shadow $9\sqrt3$. Angle with the plane?
Dihedral rays $MK = 4$, $MN = 4$ (⊥ edge), $KN = 4$. The dihedral angle?
problems.explanation: Triangle $KMN$ is equilateral ($4,4,4$) — all angles $60°$. The planes open like a book at $60°$.

steps.title: Your ladder knows this lesson
steps.body: Fire codes worldwide prescribe the ladder angle as roughly $75°$ to the ground — the '4-to-1 rule': for every 4 units up, pull the base out 1 unit. That's $\tan\theta = 4$, i.e. $\theta \approx 75.96°$. Steeper and the ladder tips backward; shallower and the base slides. It's the line-plane angle, enforced by law.

steps.title: Angles in space
bullets: Line-plane angle: between the line and its PROJECTION; sin θ = height/oblique.
Dihedral angle: rays ⊥ to the edge, one per plane, same point; measure between the rays.
Both reduce to a flat right triangle — find it, redraw it true, apply trig.
Special angles rule the exams: sin 30° = 1/2, sin 45° = √2/2, sin 60° = √3/2.

practice.statement: How many distinct planes are determined by $6$ points, no $3$ collinear and no $4$ coplanar?
$PF \perp \alpha$, $PF = 5$, and point $A$ in $\alpha$ has $FA = 12$. Find the oblique $PA$.
In cube $ABCDA'B'C'D'$, classify: (a) $AD$ and $B'C'$; (b) $AB'$ and $DC'$... just (a), plus: how many edges are skew to $BC$?
practice.solution: (a) $AD$ and $B'C'$ both run front-to-back — $B'C'$ is $BC$ lifted, and $BC \parallel AD$ — so they're **parallel**. Skew to $BC$: by the 3-4-4 split every edge has, $11 - 3\text{ (parallel)} - 4\text{ (meeting)} = 4$ skew edges.

practice.statement: From point $P$ the perpendicular to plane $\alpha$ has length $20$, and an oblique has length $25$. Find the projection of the oblique.
An oblique makes a $60°$ angle with a plane and its projection (shadow) has length $4$. Find the oblique and the height.
practice.solution: Shadow = oblique × cos: $4 = PA\cos 60° = \frac{PA}{2}$ ⟹ $PA = 8$. Height $= 8\sin 60° = 4\sqrt{3}$. Check: $4^2 + (4\sqrt3)^2 = 16 + 48 = 64 = 8^2$ ✓.

practice.statement: Two obliques from $P$ have lengths $13$ and $15$; their projections differ, but the perpendicular is the same $PF = 12$. Find both projections.
Rays $MK$ and $MN$ are drawn perpendicular to the edge of a dihedral angle at $M$, one in each face, with $MK = MN = 5$ and $KN = 5\sqrt{3}$. Find the dihedral angle.
practice.solution: Law of cosines in $\triangle KMN$: $\cos\angle M = \frac{25 + 25 - 75}{2 \cdot 25} = \frac{-25}{50} = -\frac12$ ⟹ $\angle M = 120°$. An obtuse book-opening.

practice.statement: A $6$ m pole stands perpendicular to level ground. A taut wire runs from its top to a stake $6$ m from the pole's base. What angle does the wire make with the ground, and how long is it?

testYourself.statement: Through a single line, how many planes pass? And through two INTERSECTING lines?
testYourself.solution: Through one line: infinitely many (the book's pages). Through two intersecting lines: exactly ONE — two intersecting lines determine a plane.

testYourself.statement: In the cube, name the relationship: $AB'$ (a face diagonal) and $CC'$ (a vertical edge).
testYourself.solution: $AB'$ lies in the front face and climbs left-bottom to right-top; $CC'$ is the vertical at the back-right. They don't meet (different faces, no shared vertex) and aren't parallel ($AB'$ isn't vertical) — **skew**.

testYourself.statement: An oblique is twice the perpendicular from the same point $P$. What angle does the oblique make with the plane?
A dihedral angle is $60°$. Rays ⊥ the edge at the same point have lengths $MK = 8$ and $MN = 8$. Find $KN$.
testYourself.solution: Law of cosines: $KN^2 = 64 + 64 - 2 \cdot 64 \cdot \frac12 = 64$ ⟹ $KN = 8$. (Equal rays at $60°$ make an equilateral triangle.)

testYourself.statement: A $12$-unit oblique makes a $30°$ angle with the plane. Find the height, the projection, and check them with Pythagoras.
